lines.text: Packager Split Scenarios
The Packager is the layer between semantic elements and the page. It
decides when a unit must split, where the split falls, and what state carries
forward. Three scenarios exercise the main split strategies on a short 420
× 360 pt page.
A short lead paragraph fills some vertical space so the first scenario's split
happens mid-page rather than at the very top.
§1 — Keep-With-Next Forces a Split
Expected: the lead line and the first fragment of the long paragraph appear together
on the same page; the continuation opens on the next page.
Setup: narrow column absorbs vertical space so the
keep-with-next split falls mid-page.
Lead line: this sentence is kept with the paragraph below it — the
Packager must not strand it alone at the foot of a page.
Bridge: still part of the keep-with-next chain, holding the lead to the
long paragraph.
Before movable type each book was a unique
artefact, produced by a copyist who introduced small
variations with every stroke. After Gutenberg, every
copy of a work became identical to every other copy.
The implications for knowledge transmission, for
authority, and for error were profound. Before
Split boundary — continues on next page
Split boundary — continued from previous page

Flow resumes normally after the keep-with-next chain.
§2 — Table Splits Mid-Page
Expected: the table begins below the setup text, then splits across pages with a
repeated header on the continuation.
Setup: narrow column forces extra wrapping so
the table begins mid-page.
Scenario lead: limited vertical space remains above — the table must
split even though it does not start at the page top.
Continuation note: the header row should repeat on every page
carrying table content.

§3 — Page-Top Overflow Split
Expected: the long paragraph begins at the top of a fresh page, overflows even that
empty page, and is split with a marker at the page top.
Scenario lead: the next paragraph is forced to a new page and will
overflow even an empty page.
Continuation note: the paragraph below must split at the page top —
there is no preceding content to position it mid-page.
The Roman codex replaced the scroll as the
dominant book form around the first century CE.
Unlike the scroll, whose text ran in a single
continuous column, the codex gathered leaves into
ordered quires — a structure well suited to the
page-break concept that layout engines still model
today. The Roman codex replaced the scroll as the
dominant book form around the first century CE.
